summary: Asparouhova applies Hirschman's economic theory of 'exit vs voice' to explain why online outrage has exploded: users can't leave dominant platforms due to network effects and data lock-in, so they resort to voice (outrage) instead. She argues that while regulation and platform governance reforms represent 'voice,' they paradoxically strengthen our ties to these platforms. The piece proposes two alternatives: off-platform exits (data portability, new platforms) and on-platform exits (private messengers, blocking tools, groups, Stories). She suggests that embracing 'filter bubbles' and letting communities shard into smaller groups might be healthier than forcing everyone into the same digital space, while acknowledging this could create 'knowledge ghettos' where influencers retreat from public discourse.
key_points: Platform lock-in transforms dissent into outrage—when users can't leave, they resort to voice, which manifests as the anger we see online
Regulation and platform governance reforms, while well-intentioned, actually strengthen our dependency on existing platforms rather than enabling alternatives
True platform sovereignty lies with the network (users), not the platforms themselves—Twitter and Facebook have no inherent value except as agreed-upon gathering places
On-platform exits (private messengers, blocking tools, groups, Stories) offer a more realistic path than building new platforms, essentially letting people 'shard' into smaller communities
Filter bubbles might actually be healthy—designing for smaller, context-rich communities rather than forcing everyone into the same space
money_quotes: Maybe outrage is dissent without an outlet, a byproduct of platform lock-in with nowhere to hide and nowhere else to go.
When people can't exit, they resort to voice, which looks a lot like outrage.
Twitter has no inherent value, except that it's where we agree to gather, find, and talk to each other.
By investing in voice, we're doubling down on a bad relationship instead of exploring alternatives.
Facebook isn't a community. It's a landowner: the wide plains and open skies to which communities flock.
If platforms treat 'exit' as a feature rather than a bug, they can retain their users by offering spaces for smaller, context-rich interactions.
Given our scale today, maybe bubbles aren't such a bad thing. Maybe they're just realistic.
Platforms have to choose whether to provide exit or voice, but as the de facto governments of the digital world, they also can't sit on the fence and do neither. To do nothing is autocracy.
key_insight: Online outrage is fundamentally an economic problem of platform lock-in—when users can't exit, voice becomes rage, and the solution may be designing for smaller communities rather than forcing universal discourse.
